metadata.summary: Framework for designing self-reinforcing acquisition and engagement systems where the output of one cycle becomes the input for the next, with five loop types and efficiency calculation.

content.title: Growth Loops
content.summary: A framework for designing self-reinforcing acquisition and engagement systems where the output of one cycle becomes the input for the next. Covers five loop types (viral, content, paid, network effect, supply-side), loop efficiency calculation, loop design template, and the distinction between compounding loops and linear funnels.

why.p: Linear funnels require continuous spend to maintain growth; loops compound. Each user acquired through a loop lowers the marginal cost of the next. The loop efficiency formula (product of step-conversion rates) makes the difference between viral, stable, and decaying loops mathematically explicit — so optimization targets the actual bottleneck, not the most visible step.

list.item: Product has natural sharing moments (sending links, creating public content, inviting collaborators).
Acquisition is linear and you want to reduce CAC over time.
Designing a new product surface and can choose architecture that enables a loop.
Current K-factor is measurable and you want a structured path to improve it.
Pre-PMF: designing loops before core value is validated produces loops that compound the wrong behavior.
Products with no trackable sharing or referral events — loop metrics cannot be measured.
When the loop mechanism requires deceptive or spam-adjacent behavior — brand damage exceeds the acquisition gain.
LTV:CAC < 3:1 for paid loops — the economics don't support reinvestment.

rule.statement: Design at least one growth loop before scaling acquisition spend; linear funnels require proportional spend increases to maintain growth, while loops compound output with each cycle.
rule.rationale: A funnel ends when a user converts. A loop reinvests the output (referral, content, revenue) as new input for the next cycle. The efficiency difference compounds over 12+ months into an order-of-magnitude CAC advantage.

section.name: viral-loop
section.p: Mechanism: users invite other users directly. Flow: user signs up → uses product → invites friends → friends sign up → repeat. Key metric: viral coefficient (K-factor). Examples: Dropbox "get 500MB for each friend," WhatsApp (works better with contacts), Calendly (scheduling link is sent to others). K-factor above 1.0 = viral growth; below 1.0 = requires supplemental acquisition.

section.name: content-loop
section.p: Mechanism: users create content that attracts new users via search or sharing. Flow: user creates content → content indexed and shared → new user discovers → signs up → creates content → repeat. Key metrics: content created per user, SEO traffic from user-generated content. Examples: Pinterest (boards rank in Google), Quora (Q&A ranks in search), YouTube (videos attract viewers who become creators).

section.name: paid-loop
section.p: Mechanism: revenue funds acquisition that generates more revenue. Flow: user pays → revenue → reinvest in ads → new user acquired → pays → repeat. Key metric: LTV:CAC ratio (must exceed 3:1 for sustainability). Examples: e-commerce (each sale funds more ads), SaaS (subscription revenue funds growth marketing). Requires positive unit economics before the loop is self-sustaining.

section.name: network-effect-loop
section.p: Mechanism: product becomes more valuable as more users join, reducing churn and attracting new users. Flow: user joins → product value increases → attracts more users → repeat. Key metrics: network density, engagement per user. Examples: LinkedIn, Uber (more drivers = shorter wait = more riders), Airbnb (more hosts = more options = more travelers).

section.name: supply-side-loop
section.p: Mechanism: suppliers or creators attract demand, which attracts more suppliers. Flow: creator joins → creates supply → attracts consumers → revenue → more creators join → repeat. Key metrics: creator acquisition, creator retention. Examples: Substack (writers bring audience), Shopify (stores bring customers), App Store (developers attract users).

section.name: loop-efficiency-formula

rule.statement: Calculate loop efficiency as the product of all step-conversion rates; an efficiency below 1.0 decays (requires external input), at 1.0 maintains, and above 1.0 compounds.
rule.rationale: The efficiency number makes optimization priorities explicit. Without it, teams focus on the most visible step rather than the lowest-conversion step, which is the actual bottleneck.

section.p: Formula: loop efficiency = step-1-to-2 conversion × step-2-to-3 conversion × step-3-to-1 conversion. Example: 40% × 85% × 15% × 25% = 1.3% — this loop decays and needs supplemental acquisition to maintain growth. Users after 12 months = initial users × (efficiency ^ (365 / cycle time in days)).

rule.statement: Always identify and fix the step with the lowest conversion rate before optimizing any other step; the bottleneck determines the loop's ceiling efficiency.
rule.rationale: Improving a non-bottleneck step produces negligible loop efficiency gains. If step 3-to-1 conversion is 3% and other steps are 60%+, doubling step 1-to-2 from 60% to 80% adds almost nothing; doubling step 3-to-1 from 3% to 6% doubles overall efficiency.

section.name: implementation-phases
section.p: Phase 1 — Identify (Week 1): list all ways users currently bring in other users, map natural sharing moments in the product, find user-created content, calculate current K-factor. Phase 2 — Design (Week 2): choose primary loop type, map all steps, measure conversion per step, identify bottleneck, design experiments. Phase 3 — Instrument (Week 3): set up per-step tracking, create dashboard, set baseline and targets. Phase 4 — Optimize (ongoing): run bottleneck experiments, track cycle time, track compounding effect.

section.name: loop-stack-pattern

rule.statement: Build one primary loop until it produces measurable compounding before adding a secondary loop; operating two immature loops simultaneously dilutes focus and produces results from neither.
rule.rationale: Each loop requires instrumentation, optimization experiments, and organizational attention. Slack's three-loop stack (viral, content, enterprise sales) was built sequentially over years, not launched simultaneously.

section.p: Healthy loop stack example — Slack: Primary: viral (teams invite team members). Secondary: content (integrations attract search traffic). Third: enterprise paid (revenue funds outbound sales).

example.note: Project management tool content loop. Flow: user creates project → project has public page → page ranks in Google → new visitor finds it → visitor signs up → creates own project. Metrics: 15% of users create public projects, each public project gets 50 visits/month, 3% of visitors sign up. Month 1: 1,000 users → 150 projects → 7,500 visitors → 225 new users. Month 2: 1,225 users → 184 projects → 9,200 visitors → 276 new users. The loop compounds even though K-factor is well below 1.0 — content loops can produce compounding growth without being viral.

Freelance platform viral loop. Conversions at each step: freelancer joins (100%) → gets first job (40%) → client satisfied (85%) → client refers colleague (15%) → referral signs up (25%). Loop efficiency: 0.40 × 0.85 × 0.15 × 0.25 = 1.3%. This is a decaying loop — it contributes to but does not sustain growth. Bottleneck: "client refers colleague" at 15%. Doubling referral rate to 30% doubles loop efficiency to 2.6%.

E-commerce subscription box paid loop. Customer buys ($50) → 30% profit margin = $15 → reinvest 50% = $7.50 → CPA is $5 → acquire 1.5 new customers → 1.5 × $50 = $75 revenue. Requirements for this loop to be sustainable: LTV greater than CAC, positive unit economics at current volume, ability to scale ad spend without CPM inflation eroding margins.

antipattern.description: Ignoring the quality of loop-acquired users by measuring only volume.
antipattern.reason: Viral loops that bring low-quality users (low activation, low retention) can grow the user count while damaging unit economics and product reputation. Measure activation rate of loop-acquired users separately from paid-acquired users.

antipattern.description: Forcing unnatural or incentivized sharing that feels spammy.
antipattern.reason: Forced sharing (requiring users to tweet to unlock a feature) damages brand trust and produces low-quality referrals who churn immediately. Find genuine sharing moments where the user has a natural motivation to tell someone else.

antipattern.description: Optimizing cycle time without measuring step conversions.
antipattern.reason: Faster cycles of a loop with low efficiency produce no compounding. Step conversion rates determine the ceiling; cycle time only affects how fast you reach that ceiling.

template.purpose: Loop design canvas: type, steps, conversion per step, bottleneck, optimization plan.
